data.title: Canadian Apartments: You Have To Step Up To Buy Here
data.description:
Things have not worked perfectly for our Canadian Apartments REIT (TSX:CAR.UN:CA) call. While we dodged a decent amount of the downdraft by recognizing that the stock was getting a bit ahead of the company fundamentals in September, we became bullish a little too quickly in November.

Still we dodged about $8.00 of the $13 drop from the top and that is something. We look at the recent Q4-2024 results and update our views.

Q4-2024

The first thing to notice here is that CAPREIT has been going on a tear in the repositioning strategy. We have written about this before and we saw even more activity on this front. The total number of owned suites is down more than 15,000 units and another $307 million of assets are held for sale.

Some of this has to do with their planned liquidation of European Residential REIT (ERE.UN:CA), but they are selling suites in the Netherlands and in Canada.

  • Subsequent to year-end, CAPREIT disposed of an additional 1,273 suites and sites in Canada for a total gross sale price of $213.1 million (excluding transaction costs and other adjustments), including an MHC property with 176 sites for $12.5 million. In addition, CAPREIT disposed of an additional 279 suites in the Netherlands for a total gross sale price of $83.3 million (excluding transaction costs and other adjustments).
  • Subsequent to year-end, CAPREIT acquired an additional 281 suites in Canada for a total gross purchase price of $97.6 million (excluding transaction costs and other adjustments).

What appears to be the plan here is that CAPREIT is improving the average age of the portfolio (weeding out the older buildings and buying newer ones) but overall the portfolio is getting smaller.

This strategy is being complemented by buying back its own stock at a discount.

  • During the three months ended December 31, 2024, CAPREIT purchased and cancelled approximately 6.8 million Trust Units, under the Normal Course Issuer Bid ("NCIB") program, at a weighted average purchase price of $44.37 per Trust Unit, for a total cost of $300.1 million. During the year ended December 31, 2024, CAPREIT purchased and cancelled approximately 7.3 million Trust Units, under the NCIB program, at a weighted average purchase price of $44.66 per Trust Unit, for a total cost of $327.1 million.

This is a very aggressive buyback for a REIT and shows that it believes that the NAV is truly way higher than the stock price. Moving on from the capital activity to the actual results, we saw that CAPREIT once again delivered. Same property net operating income (NOI) came in strong and the funds from operations (FFO) increased by 3.3%. The FFO payout ratio contracted slightly even though CAPEIT has delivered a distribution hike during 2024.

The key focus for investors though, has always been on the change in monthly rent. This metric was going vertical for suite turnovers in 2023 (29.9% in Q4-2023) and has now become progressively more subdued. Still, the 13.6% is a strong number and the rental market cooling off is not a bad thing. For one, it encourages turnovers. As can be seen below turnovers were 3.3% in this quarter versus in Q4-2023. The lower sticker shock contributes to this.

The higher rents and NOI contributed to an increase in NAV and a lower debt to gross book value. Interest coverage remained at a healthy 3.3X, despite 2024 marking a good increase in weighted average interest rate paid (from 2.8% to 3.11%).

One thing to remember here is that NAV is extremely subjective. We tend to look at a wide variety of opinions and calculations and even crunch some numbers ourselves to get to where the true value lies. In the case of CAPREIT, we think the company generally understates its NAV, unlike what we feel about other REITs.

CAPREIT rewarded investors with another nice hike and they have a lot of room to hike even more.

On February 13, 2025, the Board of Trustees approved an increase in monthly distributions from $0.125 to $0.1292 per Trust Unit, or from $1.50 to $1.55 per Trust Unit on an annualized basis. The increase is effective with the February 2025 distribution payable on March 17, 2025 to Unitholders of record as at February 28, 2025.

Source: CAPREIT Q4-2024 Results

Outlook & Verdict

If you want to buy an apartment and become a landlord there is no better way to do it than to buy CAPREIT. You get a diversified portfolio across Canada and most importantly, you get your properties at a 25% discount to NAV. We have identified in the chart below, the points where CAPREIT last traded at a 25% discount to NAV.

The first two produced scintillating 1 year returns and we are now back to a third. Of course, this time could be different and a sample size of two is hardly proof of anything. But the fundamentals do look very solid and we have a big tailwind with falling interest rates. As we have previously shown, CAPREIT's dividend yield (inverse of price) moves alongside Government of Canada's 5 year bond yields.

Interestingly, if CAPREIT's dividend yield matches that of the GOC-5, it would trade at $55.75, pennies higher than the current NAV. There are some known headwinds from decreased immigration but we believe it is way overblown compared to the tight supply fundamentals. In fact the biggest real headwind was briskly rising rates and CAPREIT maintained its 3.3X interest coverage despite average interest rates rising 10% in 2024. That is predominantly in the rearview mirror and forward rates are no threat to the company. CAPREIT only has 11% of debt maturing in 2025.

Q4-2024 refinancings were at 3.67% and current refinance rates are even lower (3.3%). We are upgrading this to a Strong Buy and see a total return potential of 30% over two years from here.

Please note that this is not financial advice. It may seem like it, sound like it, but surprisingly, it is not. Investors are expected to do their own due diligence and consult a professional who knows their objectives and constraints.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Morgan Stanley: The Outlook Isn't The Same (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
Morgan Stanley's (NYSE:MS) stock has continued to perform well in 2025 after 2024, where the shares appreciated approximately 35% and outperformed the S&P 500 by around 10%. So far in the year, the shares are up 8.54%, with most of the gains occurring after the release of the Q4 earnings.

In my previous ratings of Morgan Stanley, I have rated the firm as a "buy," mainly due to its strong momentum in its core business, Wealth Management, and also because of a rebound in capital markets. In Q4, the strong earnings continued, and the company obtained a stunning EPS surprise of 32.13% and a revenue surprise of 7.92%. These metrics came in at $2.22 EPS and $16.22 billion in revenue, setting quarterly firm records.

Although these surprises were spectacular, MS's closest peer, Goldman Sachs (GS), had bigger surprises. Both companies benefitted from a volatile Q4 in markets where trading desks obtained higher volumes due to macro factors such as unexpectedly rising long-term yields and a presidential election.

Q4 Earnings

Morgan Stanley has three segments: Institutional Securities, Wealth Management, and Investment Management. In Q4, all three had strong revenue growth compared to the preceding quarters' averages.

Institutional Securities, for example, saw a stunning 47.2% rise year over year, and its $7.267 billion net revenues weighted approximately 45% of the net revenues. The trading business saw a whopping 51% rise in Equities and 35% in Fixed Income, while Investment Banking saw a 25% YoY rise thanks to higher M&A transactions and IPOs as companies took advantage of the high valuations to raise equity in public markets.

Wealth Management was the most significant revenue contributor in Q4, with $7.478 billion, or approximately 46% of the net revenues. Here, the growth arrived predominantly with higher asset values, and net interest income was basically unchanged as higher yields on the investment portfolio were offset by lower sweep deposits.

Last year, Investment Management's net revenues were $1.643 billion, a 12% rise, thanks to higher market valuations and $4.3 billion in long-term net flows, bringing the assets under management to $1.666 billion.

Overall, the investment bank made a great effort to control expenses, as the efficiency ratio passed from 84% a year ago to 69% in the most recent quarter. Finally, the return on tangible common equity, a key KPI to value banks, increased significantly to 20.2% in Q4 2024 compared to 8.4% in Q3 2023.

Valuation

Based on a share price of $137.31 and a tangible book value per share of $44.57, Morgan Stanley's stock trades expensively at 3.08x price to tangible equity. A year ago, this figure was 2.1x when the shares were at $85.89 and the tangible book value was $40.89. This multiple expansion came in predominantly from the numerator growing 6.7x faster than the denominator and a minimum share count change of 1.2%.

Historically, Morgan Stanley has traded at a slightly higher multiple than Goldman Sachs. Yet, the discrepancy has become more extensive, with GS trading at 2.06x price to tangible book value based on a share price of $650.53 and a tangible book value per share of $316.02.

A higher return on tangible book value, with MS achieving a ROTE of 20.2% in Q4 and GS 15.5%, could justify Morgan Stanley's premium over Goldman Sachs. Still, this large discrepancy has made Goldman Sachs more attractive than Morgan Stanley.

UBS Hold $130 → $140 1/21/2025
Barclays Buy $155 → $156 1/17/2025
B of A Securities Strong Buy $146 → $153 1/17/2025
Evercore ISI Group Buy $140 → $150 1/17/2025
J.P. Morgan Hold $108 → $124 1/17/2025
Keefe, Bruyette & Woods Hold $138 → $145 1/17/2025

Source: Briefing.com

Furthermore, all sell-side analysts updated their price targets on Morgan Stanley's stock upwards after earnings. Nonetheless, half of them have a neutral outlook, while the rest have a bullish outlook. At the same time, the average rating of $144.67 is close to the actual share price of approximately $137.31, providing a low margin of safety of 5.4%.

Risks

With the FED remaining in "no rush" to adjust policy rates, the main exogenous factors that moved financial stocks higher in 2023 and 2024 would probably not be the same, at least in the first two quarters. Fiscal policy adjustments seem to be driving the markets, particularly from uncertainty regarding the threat of tariffs. Although President Trump's election has boosted bank stocks with the intent of lowering regulations, tariff uncertainty could back some pending deals, especially within global M&A.

At the same time, President Trump intends to lower the tax benefits from carried interest, which is used predominantly by alternative asset managers to classify some of their earnings as capital gains and pay lower taxes than their ordinary income. Although within the Investment Management segment, performance-based income only brought $234 million in revenue in 2024, this new tax plan could affect Morgan Stanley's alternative investment earnings and the distributable earnings of some of their private market clients.

Conclusion

The financials of Morgan Stanley have been on a strong momentum thanks in particular to the growth in the Institutional Securities segment, which covers investment banking and trading. Nonetheless, given the fiscal uncertainty, a Fed in no rush, and soaring long-term yields, the outlook is perhaps not the same as it used to be six months ago when the market expected long-term yields to lower considerably and benefit the deal activity. At the same time, with a valuation of 3.08x price to tangible book value and sell-side analysts' price targets close to the actual price, I believe it is prudent to downgrade my rating from a "buy" to a "hold."

data.title: Gold And Silver Rally: The Sprott Physical Gold And Silver Trust Owns The Metals
data.description:
Gold (XAUUSD:CUR) and silver (XAGUSD:CUR) became more precious in 2024, as the precious metals posted 27.47% and 21.41% respective gains. In January 2025, the bullish price action continued, with February COMEX gold futures rising 6.49% and reaching a record high. While silver remains well below its 2011 and 1980 record peaks, the March COMEX futures price rose 10.34% in January as silver outperformed gold on a percentage basis.

The most direct route for a risk position in gold and silver is the physical market for bars and coins. The futures arena provides physical delivery mechanisms, and plenty of ETF and ETN products move higher and lower with gold and silver prices.

The Sprott Physical Gold and Silver Trust (NYSEARCA:CEF) is a liquid exchange-traded commodity fund that owns the metals.

New Highs In Gold

Gold’s bull market began in 1999 when the Bank of England auctioned half the U.K.’s gold holdings.

The CME’s quarterly continuous COMEX gold futures chart highlights the pattern of higher lows and higher highs since 1999. Gold rose above the 1980 $875 peak in 2008. The precious metal eclipsed the $1,000 level that year and has not traded below $1,000 since 2009. Gold of April 2025 delivery reached its most recent high of $2,968.50 in February 2025 as the bullish trend continues.

While gold continues to make new nominal highs, the inflation-adjusted 1980 $875 high in 2024 is above the $3,370 per ounce level, which could be the current upside target as gold’s rally continues.

Silver’s Bullish Trend Continues

In 1980, silver futures reached a record $50.36 per ounce peak. In 2011, a rally took silver just below that level to $49.82.

The quarterly continuous contract chart highlights silver’s bullish trend since the 2020 pandemic-inspired $11.64 per ounce low. At near $33 per ounce in mid-February, silver has nearly tripled in value over the past five years.

Interestingly, the silver inflation-adjusted 1980 $50.36 high would translate to a $194.15 per ounce price in February 2025.

Tariff Threats Have Been Bullish For The Metals

During the 2024 presidential campaign, President Trump made no secret about his preference for tariffs over taxes. The 47th U.S. President did not wait long to announce tariffs on three of the U.S.’s largest trading partners. In early February, the Trump administration leveled 25% tariffs on neighboring Canada and Mexico and an additional 10% tariff on China. The three countries are leading precious metals producers.

As the chart illustrates, China was the leading gold producer in 2024, followed by Canada in fourth place and Mexico in seventh.

In 2023, the leading silver-producing countries were:

Mexico was the leading producer, with nearly twice the silver output of second-place China. Canada was the 17th leading silver-producing country.

Tariffs distort commodity prices as they create trade barriers. Gold and silver’s most liquid markets are in London, as precious metals dealers offer allocated and unallocated gold and silver trading. The allocated precious metals have specific bar numbers in vaults. The unallocated precious metals are a book entry backed by physical holdings. London is the home of the wholesale physical bullion market, regulated by the Bank of England. The COMEX futures market in the United States has a delivery mechanism and U.S. vaults. The uncertainty of tariffs on gold and silver flowing into the U.S. has created significant volatility in EFP transactions, or the difference between prices for gold and silver in London and futures prices in the United States. Gold has been in a bullish trend since 1999, and silver since 2020. The tariff threat has only supported the bullish path of least resistance.

Respect The Trends As They Are Your Best Friends

Trends are a trader or investor’s best friend. Gold and silver are precious metals, but they offer market participants a choice. Gold is the world’s oldest means of exchange, held by governments worldwide as a foreign exchange reserve asset, validating its role in the global financial system. Gold is a highly liquid asset, and its price appreciation has been steady. Monthly historical volatility in gold is at the 10.51% level.

Silver has a long history as a means of exchange, but it is far more volatile than gold, experiencing periodic boom-and-bust price action. Silver’s monthly price variance metric is at the 25.58% level, nearly two and one-half times gold’s volatility metric. Back in the day, governments held silver as a reserve asset. However, silver’s penchant for volatility has made it a far more speculative market than gold, with most governments and central banks shunning silver. Meanwhile, silver has a myriad of industrial applications aside from its role as an investment metal and store of value. Over the past years, rising gold prices and increasing industrial and investment demand for silver have supported the price of the second-leading precious metal.

CEF Owns Gold And Silver Bullion

The fund summary for the CEF ETF product states:

As the summary highlights, CEF is “domiciled in Canada,” which could impact the economics of tariffs. CEF’s top holdings include:

CEF invests approximately two-thirds of its assets in physical gold and the rest in physical silver bullion.

At around $26.60 per share, CEF had $5.37 billion in assets under management. The CEF ETF trades an average of over 530,000 shares daily and charges around a 0.50% management fee.

The monthly chart highlights a 24% rise in 2024 and an 8.75% increase in January 2025, as CEF, with its two-thirds gold and one-third silver composition, does an excellent job tracking gold and silver prices.

The bullish trends in gold and silver show no sign of ending in February 2025. CEF is an ETF product that effectively exposes investors to the leading precious metals.

data.title: Blue Bird Stock Is A Bargain, Not A Value Trap
data.description:
Intro & Thesis

I initiated my investment coverage of Blue Bird Corporation (NASDAQ:BLBD) in mid-October last year, calling its stock extremely cheap. I was waiting for another leg higher driven by overall valuation repricing and growth prospects flowing from continuing demand for Blue Bird's key selling products (buses) with plenty of incremental growth from EV development. Unfortunately for my bullish rating, the stock continued to correct from there, quite significantly underperforming the broader stock market:

At the time, the stock was trading at just 7.7x next year's EV/EBITDA ratio — today that ratio is down to ~6.96x, according to Seeking Alpha Premium's data. I think BLBD stock isn't a value trap, as it simply doesn't fit the definition. The company seems to have a tremendous opportunity coming from its solid backlog of orders and strong funding. I reiterate my previous “Strong Buy,” thinking of BLBD as one of the most interesting picks among small caps as of today.

Why Do I Think So?

Let's first begin with a brief business description for those who don't know much about Blue Bird. The firm was founded in 1927 in Macon, Georgia, and it's currently a $1.16-billion market cap company that “designs, engineers, produces and sells school buses in the US, Canada, and internationally.” Their business is split into Buses and Parts with Type C, Type D, and specialty buses powered by propane, gasoline, compressed natural gas, electric, and diesel engines. Blue Bird also offers spare parts, loans, and extended warranties not only for buses — its products are sold via a dealer network, direct sales to fleet owners and government agencies, and a parts center.

The firm reported for its fiscal Q1 FY2025 on February 5th, 2025, and the stock price increased only slightly from there. So let's take a look at the results now.

Making $313.9 million in consolidated sales for the quarter (-1.2% YOY), the company recorded $28.7 million in net income and ~$45.6 million in adjusted EBITDA, beating the consensus (on the EPS level) and the internal guidance (on the adjusted EBITDA level, Blue Bird had its second-highest Q1 ever). In addition to it, BLBD's adjusted EBITDA margin declined to 14.6% from Q1 FY2024's 15%, but last year's figure was the absolute record for them. Thus, effectively, Blue Bird is showing signs of doing an impressive job as it supports the “peak marginality levels” many investors feared would come down meaningfully from 2025 on.

Looking at the management's guidance dynamics, we can see that a) BLBD has beaten the guidance on every reported metric and b) the next few quarters should be exceptionally strong as the top-line keeps growing by ~$25 million on a QoQ basis:

As you can see, the management forecasts ~13.55% in adjusted EBITDA margin for the rest of the fiscal FY2025 (or ~13.79% for full-year 2025), which is ~103 bps below the Q1 level. I think we can't rule out the possibility that the management intentionally lowered Blue Bird's potential EBITDA levels to temper the Wall Street analysts' expectations and be able to beat earnings estimates again as new quarters come.

Be as it may, during Q1 we saw that BLBD's average selling prices (for their ICE buses) increased by ~6% YOY, but because of the overall average vehicle selling price slightly decreasing (due to the lower EV mix), the top line showed a little decline on a YOY basis. But we see that for Q2 the management sees $350 in sales, implying an increase of ~1.2% compared to Q2 FY2024. While it might seem like something insignificant, I think the top line might increase more dramatically because a) the backlog has grown and b) the firm is ready to “implement a 5% price increase on non-EV bus orders to offset potential tariffs on imported components from Canada and Mexico.” [Source.]

Also, I believe the overall long-term effect on adjusted EBITDA should be very positive as the management said on the earnings call that they're still targeting 14.5-15% in adjusted EBITDA margin by 2028:

Starting in 2028 and beyond, our long-term target remains to drive profitable growth to higher levels towards $1.85 billion to $2 billion in revenue, comprising of 11,000 to 12,000 units and generate EBITDA of $270 million to $300 million plus or 14.5% to 15% plus at best in class level.

The fact is that they're already in that range as of Q1.

On the earnings call, they also addressed the uncertainty caused by the President's Executive Order pausing EPA funding, which is the likely reason for BLBD stock's correction, in my understanding. As some of you might recall, some investment banks downgraded BLBD in early October 2024 on “worries that delay in [EPA] funding disbursement could negatively impact Blue Bird's growth.” As the Q2 numbers and comments revealed, the fact is that Blue Bird's EV backlog keeps growing:

... despite the EPA subsequently allowing extensions to that EV order deadline, we saw a spike in our EV orders with the backlog at the end of Q1 standing at a record 765 EVs, and that's 22% higher than at the end of fiscal 2024 and a substantial 82% above the same time a year ago.

The tailwind that might have covered the EPA's negatives is the late-breaking news of the court ruling against pausing fund disbursements under the Infrastructure and Investment Jobs Act and the Inflation Reduction Act. This is a net-net positive for Blue Bird's EV sales and DOE grant for plant expansion.

So Blue Bird's growth is unlikely to get stalled during 2025.

Overall, at this point, I see no signs that would suggest that BLBD is a value trap. I know it might seem as such because it's been cheap for a while. But as it goes from Morningstar's explanation of the term, I think that the underlying cause for Blue Bird stock's correction isn't accompanied by anything “structurally disruptive emerging” that can prevent it from achieving the guidance its management has set for themselves. Blue Bird simply doesn't fit into the general definition of the term “value trap” from what I can see today.

Assuming that the firm closes FY2025 with 14% in adjusted EBITDA margin (again, that would be below Q1's 14.58%) and its sales arrive at the consensus $1.44 billion (although the management's mid-range guidance hints at $1.45 billion), the stock's EV/EBITDA ratio will be ~5.55x by the end of FY2025. Given that the firm is quite active in share repurchases (they bought ~$10 million worth of stock during Q1 and have $40 million remaining under the current program), I think BLBD has every chance of achieving at least ~7.5x in EV/EBITDA (on an adjusted basis) by the end of calendar 2025 (or maybe 2026). Why? Multiple expansion is oftentimes stimulated by share count reductions.

So at 7.5x EV/EBITDA, the stock is currently ~35% undervalued, which naturally leads me to reiterate my previous “Strong Buy” rating.

Risks And Final Thoughts

Of course, my thesis isn't riskless: One of the most prominent issues that I mentioned in my article is BLBD's reliance on government funding, particularly the EPA's Clean School Bus Program. The EPA funding delays and the overall uncertainty in the disbursement and continuation of these government incentives directly impact Blue Bird's projected EV sales and overall financials in the span of the next few years. So the management's guidance may not be realized, and in this case, the implied EBITDA figures will be lower than what I'm assuming in today's analysis.

Anyway, if we look at the facts and latest trends, Blue Bird's business momentum is still there. The underlying forces that should drive up the demand for BLBD's offerings — the aging fleet of the school buses in the US — are likely to lead the current guidance proving right. I mean, Blue Bird is clearly having longer-term tailwinds from the end market today while at the same time, its marginality is likely to stay elevated thanks to a better revenue mix in the future (more EV sales). Even if the EPA funding isn't there, today's valuation of the stock suggests a comfortable upside potential from here.

I decided to reiterate my previous “Strong Buy” rating on BLBD.

Thank you for reading!

data.title: Tesla: Bears Will Regret Overlooking Shanghai Megapack Factory (Upgrade)
data.description:
TSLA stock: previous thesis and new developments

My last work on Tesla, Inc. (NASDAQ:TSLA) gave the stock a Sell rating. My last article was entitled “Tesla: Lessons I Learned From Stocks With 100+ P/E (Rating Downgrade)” and was published in mid-January. In that article, I presented my assessment of the stock’s return/risk analysis based on the lessons that I’ve learned about high P/E stocks. Quote:

Since my last writing, a few additional growth catalysts have emerged for Tesla, Inc. They offer TSLA opportunities to further broaden its EV lineup, AI-enabled products, and lower unit costs. Furthermore, its aggressive CAPEX reinvestment inflates its accounting P/E (172x). But even adjusted for owners' earnings, the P/E remains elevated and has already priced in the expected growth for years, or even a decade, to come.

Since then, there have been a few important changes surrounding TSLA. First, the stock prices have pulled back substantially, as you can see from the following chart.

A few days ago (February 11th to exact), its stock price corrected almost a quarter (-24%) off its level at the time of my writing. The prices recovered somewhat afterward, but are still about -17% off the level at my previous writing. With such a price pullback, I consider the valuation risks largely dissipated in the near term and my previous sell rating obsolete by this time.

Meanwhile, another critical catalyst in my view – which is the focus of this article — has developed. According to a Reuters report on Feb. 10, Tesla's Shanghai Megapack battery plant just launched production (and more details of this plant are provided in the news report below). Judging by the recent articles published on TSLA, this catalyst has been largely overlooked by Seeking Alpha analysts. In the remainder, I analyze the potential impacts of this analyst and explain why investors – especially the bears — will regret overlooking it. This catalyst, combined with the price pullback, has also promoted me to change my rating on the stock to BUY.

Energy Storage News: Tesla has started trial production at its Megapack assembly plant in Shanghai, China, state-owned news reported this week. It is the electric vehicle (EV) and battery energy storage system (BESS) firm’s second major manufacturing facility dedicated to producing its grid-scale Megapack BESS product, after its existing facility in Lathrop, California. Mass production at the Shanghai site is expected to begin in the first quarter of 2025, the company told Xinhua News Agency (New China News Agency), claiming it was built in record time. Its initial capacity will be 10,000 Megapacks a year, or 40GWh of energy storage capacity, and Tesla invested around US$200 million (1.45 billion CNY) into it.

TSLA stock: What is a Megapack?

This catalyst is overlooked for understandable reasons. TSLA’s Megapack business enjoyed little spotlight compared to its EVs – at least thus far. So, I feel a brief introduction is necessary here. To put it simply, a Megapack is a giant rechargeable battery. Renewable energy sources like solar and wind power sometimes produce more electricity and sometimes less generating, making them unstable. With an energy storage system – when it is sufficient large — you can store excess electricity during peak production and release it when needed. The keyword here is “sufficient large” and the chart below provides a sense of scale of how large (and expensive) these Megapacks are.

Taking the Megapack 2 XL as an example, a single one of them has an energy storage capacity of up to 3.9 megawatt-hours and an inverter power of more than 1.9 megawatts as seen. To better contextualize these figures, these numbers mean that a Megapack 2 XL can meet the electricity need of about 3,600 households for one hour or fully charge 65 Model 3 vehicles.

So far, it sounds like a basic idea. But the potential is far more than what seems on the surface, as to be detailed next.

TSLA stock: bears underestimated Megapack impacts

In early 2019, Musk predicted that Tesla's energy storage business would exceed the scale of the automotive business. Of course, you should not believe everything Musk says, especially in terms of timescale. However, in the case here, I agree with his prediction. I see so many nonlinear growth opportunities here. The addressable markets are huge, ranging from household to large data centers as the world gradually shifts toward renewables. Besides being a key element in our renewable energy future, energy storage can also participate in peak shaving and generate arbitrage profits and integrates with TSLA’s other segments nicely (such as its charting/service stations).

Before the Shanghai Megapack factory, Tesla already has a mega-factory in Lathrop, California to make them. The opening of the Shanghai factory is a clear indication of the growth potential in this area in my view. The next chart shows Tesla's financial summary for its least quarter (Q4 of FY 2024). As seen, total automotive revenues decreased by 8% YOY and landed at $19,798 million in Q4. However, energy generation and storage revenue saw triple-digit growth, more than doubling to $3,061 million in the same period.

Admittedly, the sales from energy generation and storage are still only a fraction of its automotive segment (another reason for it being overlooked). However, a $3.0 billion segment is not negligible by any stretch, especially the number grows at triple-digits rates.

Looking ahead, I expect such rapid growth to continue. I also expect higher margins from this segment compared to its EVs sales with the addition of the Shanghai factory. Compared with the U.S., China's policy environment is relatively relaxed, the industrial chain is well-equipped and matured, and labor costs can be better controlled. Morgan Stanley recently predicted that Tesla's energy storage business alone will be worth about $130 billion U.S. dollars (see the chart below). My estimate based on Tesla’s share count today shows that this translates into about $40 dollars of shares price alone. Given the demand for energy storage and the competitive advantage its Shanghai factory just added, I am very optimistic about these projections.

Other risks and final thoughts

Since this article focused on TSLA’s energy storage, I should point out that its EV segment is still earning the bread and butter for now. And the competition in the EV space is intensifying. Established automakers are all rapidly electrifying their lineups, and new EV startups are emerging, all competing with TSLA for market share.

In the battery energy storage system (“BESS”) market, the competition is also heating up. Other established companies (such as BYD and ABB) and recent entrants (notably Fluence) are also actively pursuing this lucrative market. Fluence specializes in energy storage solutions and is rapidly building a strong track record in deploying large-scale BESS projects. But my assessment is that TSLA is still the leading player in this space, and I expect the opening of its Shanghai factor to further widen and consolidate its competitive advantages in terms of both scale and cost.

Finally, Tesla's reliance on its outspoken CEO, Elon Musk, presents a unique key person risk – whether it is an upside or down risk depends on the individual investor’s perspective.

To conclude, the goal of this article is to upgrade my rating on TSLA stock to BUY from my earlier sell rating. The key considerations behind this upgrade are twofold. Firstly, the price pullback since my last writing has largely dissipated the valuation risk I saw in the near term and thus obsoleted my earlier thesis. Secondly and more importantly, Tesla just launched production at its Shanghai Megapack battery plant. Judging by the recent articles on the Seeking Alpha platform, I feel this is a catalyst that many investors overlooked and/or underestimated. I urge them to include this factor in their TSLA consideration given the nonlinear growth potential ahead.

data.title: Agnico Eagle Mines Limited 2024 Q4 - Results - Earnings Call Presentation
data.description: The following slide deck was published by Agnico Eagle Mines Limited in conjunction with their 2024 Q4 earnings call.

data.title: Are Critical Minerals Trump Card In U.S.-China Chip Showdown?
data.description:
On Dec. 3, 2024, China's Ministry of Commerce implemented export bans on key semiconductor materials, including gallium and germanium, to the US. While these minerals had already been subject to existing export restrictions introduced in July 2023, this is the first time China has specifically targeted the US. To date, US legislation has focused on strengthening the downstream part of the semiconductor supply chain, leaving US technologies vulnerable to upstream supply chain disruption.

The Take

US-China tensions centered on semiconductors and critical minerals are apt to continue rising as each government deploys the levers at its disposal, including restrictions on the upstream supply of materials key to the chip sector. Despite the Trump administration's opposition to many of the Biden administration's policies, measures targeting critical minerals supply chains, particularly onshoring efforts, will remain front and center. However, the extent and scope of changes to be rolled out in the coming months is still unknown.

Tensions rise as US, China seek technological superiority

By the end of December 2024, the Biden administration initiated a formal trade investigation into China's semiconductor industry, underscoring the ongoing trade and technology tensions, or so-called chip war, between the world's two largest economies.

The investigation will be conducted under Section 301 of the Trade Act of 1974 and aims to identify unfair and unethical practices as levers to support revamping its own domestic industry. Days before the presidential transition in January 2025, Biden's administration also unveiled an export control regime that gives 20 close allies and partners uninhibited access to AI-related chips, while placing licensing requirements on most other countries. The policy aims to make it harder for China to use other countries to circumvent existing US restrictions to access chip technology. The execution and outcome of both provisions will be the responsibility of the new Trump administration.

Semiconductors are essential components of electronic devices, controlling the flow of electric currents in applications such as quantum computing, AI and other advanced technologies. They are highly material intensive and have a complex manufacturing process.

The US has made significant investments in recent years to build semiconductor manufacturing capacity, positioning itself as a global leader in fabless manufacturing. By avoiding the capital-intensive fabrication stage, fabless companies such as US-based Nvidia Corp. (NVDA) and Advanced Micro Devices Inc. (AMD) outsource semiconductor manufacturing to focus on design and innovation. Semiconductor manufacturing and assembly is now concentrated in Asia, with Taiwan, China and South Korea accounting for over 80% of the global supply of finished products. Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Co. Ltd. (TSM) is the largest manufacturer of advanced chips, with a greater than 50% market share of global production.

Despite the US' entrepreneurial function, the chip shortage during the COVID-19 pandemic revealed the country’s tenuous position in the semiconductor supply chain. The US has since passed a number of legislative responses to address this imbalance.

The Creating Helpful Incentives to Produce Semiconductors for America (CHIPS) and Science Act of 2022 is the largest US policy mechanism to expand investment in this field, promising $52 billion in grants, loans and other aid to onshore semiconductor research, development and production. In September 2023, the US Commerce Department issued a rule to prevent CHIPS funding from being used directly or indirectly to benefit foreign entities of concern (such as China).

Semiconductor raw materials supply chain remains concentrated

However, there is no provision in the CHIPS Act to incentivize the diversification of critical mineral supply chains for semiconductors, leaving the US - and most other Western countries - highly dependent on a handful of other nations for raw material supply. In addition to holding a monopoly over the manufacturing and assembly stages, China also has a majority share of the primary production (mining and/or refining) of several critical minerals that are essential components of semiconductors.

Arsenic, cobalt, copper, fluorine, iridium, gallium, germanium, palladium, phosphorus, scandium, other rare earth elements, high-purity silicon and titanium all contribute in varying quantities, across the many stages of the manufacturing process, to make a single chip.

Of these materials, gallium, germanium and silicon are considered the highest-risk minerals for the semiconductor industry. These three minerals are the most effective semiconductor materials and are used in the greatest quantities compared to others, such as arsenic, which are only used in trace amounts. Additionally, the primary driver for gallium, germanium and silicon is the chip industry, while demand for other named minerals is driven by other sectors, such as automotive, aerospace or construction. From a supply security perspective, the production of gallium, germanium and silicon are all highly geographically concentrated while also lacking suitable substitutes for this particular application.

While the CHIPS Act has succeeded in supporting new domestic chip manufacturing projects, the industry is mounting pressure on policymakers to address critical mineral supply chain vulnerabilities. China's move to implement an export ban of gallium and germanium to the US was a stark reminder of the risk of being dependent on any single country.

Just as the Inflation Reduction Act of 2022 spurred investments in electric vehicle mineral supply chains, US semiconductor companies and industry groups are advocating for a set of tax incentives for mining, processing and refining projects, either domestically or in allied nations, aligned to semiconductor demand.

Despite President Trump's criticism of the Democrats' legislation, his approach to decoupling from China aligns broadly with the objectives of both the Inflation Reduction Act and the CHIPS Act. In contrast to Biden's "friendshoring" approach to critical minerals, demonstrated by alliances such as the US-led Minerals Security Partnership (MSP), Trump is expected to follow an onshoring approach, with policy measures focused on building domestic mineral capacity. This would be welcome news for US chip companies such as Micron Technology Inc. (MU), Intel Corp. (INTC) and Microchip Technology Inc. (MCHP), who have committed to major projects under the CHIPS Act.

In 2024, South Korea became the first member country to succeed the US in chairing the MSP, which - as the second leading producer of semiconductors globally - should bolster the connection between access to critical minerals and economic security for manufacturers. Canada, despite facing the prospect of new tariffs, is also keen to leverage its mineral resources and has proposed a deeper critical mineral alliance with the US to reduce dependency on China.

Entering his second term in office in January 2025, Trump is expected to take a relatively hawkish approach to China and has made his stance on tariffs clear. How he decides to handle the two actions focused on semiconductors introduced by former President Biden in the remaining days of his administration will provide an early indication of his approach and the extent to which he is likely to make semiconductors a focus in US-China relations.

[1] The production concentration indicator (PCI) characterizes the level of production concentration for each material at the mining stage and, where data is available, at the refining stage. Each share is multiplied by an ESG indicator to reflect the additional risk represented by countries with lower ESG standards. More information on the PCI can be found in the British Geological Survey's criticality methodology.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Ameren Corporation 2024 Q4 - Results - Earnings Call Presentation
data.description: The following slide deck was published by Ameren Corporation in conjunction with their 2024 Q4 earnings call.

data.title: Crocs Q4: Post-Earnings 20% Rally Is Just The Beginning
data.description:
Introduction & Investment Thesis

I last wrote about Crocs (NASDAQ:CROX) in November, where I reiterated my “buy” rating with a price target of $131.

While the stock has underperformed the S&P 500 during this period of time, it is up 24% on its Q4 FY24 earnings, where its total revenue grew 3.1% YoY to $990M, driven by outperformance in its Crocs brand, while the HEYDUDE brand posted flat year-over-year revenue after several quarters of contraction.

Meanwhile, its profitability picture was mixed with its gross margins improving 260 basis points to 57.9%, while its operating margin contracted by 160 basis points to 20.2% as a result of expanding SG&A expenses to drive long-term investments.

When it comes to its Crocs brand, its Classic Clog remains the hero, while the company continues to innovate across its franchises and product lines that also include Sandals and Jibbitz to expand its market share. What is also important to note is that the management is doubling down on its International markets, where China now ranks the largest after its North American markets, with Q4 growth accelerating from its Q3 levels.

Meanwhile, its HEYDUDE brand is also showing signs of optimism with its revenue bottoming as it is successful in driving full-price selling.

Looking forward, the management expects revenue growth to slow in its Crocs brand while expecting tariff-related profitability headwinds. However, I believe that the tariff-related headwind is priced into the guidance and the stock price. Assessing both the “good” and the “bad,” I believe there to be room for at least 20% upside from its current levels with a price target of $134, making it a “buy.”

Crocs Brand: Classic Clog remains the hero, while growth in International markets accelerates.

Starting with the Crocs brand, it saw its revenue grow 4.0% on a year-over-year basis in Q4 to $762M, contributing over 76% to Total revenue. For the full year FY24, the Crocs brand grew 8.8% YoY, with its DTC (Direct-to-Consumer) channel growing at a faster rate than Wholesale. In the meantime, total volume sold increased 6% for the whole year, while ASPs (Average Selling Price) grew 2% to $25.52 as a result of favorable pricing, channel, and product mix.

During the earnings call, the management outlined the strategic pillars of its Crocs brand, which I believe is worth discussing below.

The first pillar is centered on driving brand relevance through icon iterations, where clogs make up 75% of their sales mix, as they saw sales grow 10%, led by the Classic Clog and outperformance in its International markets, while simultaneously introducing new clog franchises and reiterating a strong pipeline for 2025.

Moving on to the second pillar, the management is focused on expanding the market share of its Crocs brand outside of clogs through new wearing occasions. This is how the management breaks down its TAM (total addressable market) below, which it estimates to be $160B in total, consisting of Casual Footwear, Sandals, and Clogs.

With Sandals and Jibbitz (in their Crocs product pillar) consisting of 13% and 8% of their total business, they grew at a slower rate than Clogs at 3% and 6%, respectively. Particularly, when it comes to Jibbitz, the management is focused on driving deeper penetration in digital and wholesale channels, accelerating speed to market, and introducing fresh products with agility.

Its final pillar is centered on gaining market share across the globe. This is how the management segments its geographies based on its existing market share to drive relevant go-to-market strategies. The geographic segments include 1) Growing, 2) Emerging, and 3) Established.

While North America is its largest market for its Crocs brand, contributing close to 62% of revenue in Q4, we can see that its International markets are growing at a faster pace, and we should expect it to contribute a higher portion to Total revenue in the coming years. Particularly, when it comes to its Chinese market, it saw a growth rate of 64% YoY for the whole year, with the fourth quarter revenue accelerating from the third quarter as a result of positive comparable store sales, digital, and new store growth. In 2025, the management is looking to expand its international footprint in China, India, Southeast Asia, and the Middle East and believes that a majority of their dollar growth will come from international markets in the coming years.

HEYDUDE shows further signs of revenue bottoming as ASPs continue to tick higher.

Moving on to its HEYDUDE, its revenues were flat YoY at $228M, driven by outperformance in its DTC channel. This is better than what the management had projected in its Q3 FY24 earnings, where it expected the segment’s revenue to decline 3-4% YoY.

In my previous post, I had discussed that despite investor pessimism for Crocs’s HEYDUDE brand, it was starting to see early signs of revenue bottoming, as it posted a sequential increase in revenue growth of 3%, despite a year-over-year decline of 17.4%. In Q4, HEYDUDE revenue grew over 11% on a sequential basis, which I believe demonstrates the progress they have made in prioritizing brand health through a combination of better segmentation, pricing strategies, and content strategies.

Meanwhile, the company is focusing its efforts on building a brand community, where they have expanded their following on social channels, while assembling high-profile influencers to drive relevant brand engagement, especially among the younger female consumer, where they saw a 160% growth in new customers in the female 18-24-year-old demographic.

At the same time, it is also driving product innovation in their core Wendy and Wally assortment that continue to see growing sales traction through full-price selling with ASPs growing 7%, marking the sixth consecutive quarter of ASP growth as they stabilize their North American market while laying the groundwork for future international growth.

Tariffs to impact FY25 profitability along with slower revenue growth

Looking forward to FY25, revenue growth is expected to slow down to 2-2.5% YoY, which includes a $60M FX headwind. Breaking it down by brands, its Crocs brand is expected to grow 4.5% led by its International markets, while revenue from HEYDUDE is expected to decline at a slower rate of 7-9%.

From a profitability standpoint, the management expects the gross margins for the full year to be down slightly from currency headwinds and tariffs, while its operating margin is also expected to decline to 24%, down from 24.9% in FY24.

On the topic of tariffs, I believe it is worth diving into it in a little more detail. During the earnings call, the management outlined that its forward guidance includes a 10% tariff on goods imported from China into the US, as well as the anticipated additional 25% tariff on goods imported from Mexico, beginning in March, with the assumption that the tariffs will be in place for the remainder of the fiscal year. As a result, the management has embedded an $11M tariff-related headwind to gross profit, which is equivalent to 25 basis points to their margin rate as well as a 60 basis point headwind to their operating margin.

However, these are estimates, and the impact on the overall demand landscape is unknown. What I am trying to say is that should demand for its Crocs and HEYDUDE brands get impacted worse than expected, we may see its profitability further worsen in FY25. Having said that, the management is committed to maintaining an operating margin at or above 24% over the longer term, assuming the headwinds from currency and tariffs normalize.

Revisiting my valuation: Room for 20% higher

Looking forward, I will take the management’s forward revenue guidance for FY25 while taking consensus estimates for FY26 and FY27, which point to a slight revenue acceleration in the 4% YoY range. I believe that Crocs should be able to achieve the consensus growth targets, especially as its Crocs brand sees its faster-growing International markets contributing a higher share of Total Revenue, while its HEYDUDE brand returns to growth. This should translate to a total revenue of $4.5B by FY27.

From a profitability standpoint, I will also take the management's FY25 guidance for Adjusted Operating Margin at 24% and assume it will remain at the level over the next two years as the management has discussed, which will translate to a total Adjusted Operating income of $1.08B by FY27, which will be equivalent to a present value of $932M when discounted at 8%.

Assuming a forward price-to-earnings ratio of 8.5, which is less than half of the average price-to-earnings multiple of the S&P 500 companies, given the growth rate of its earnings during this period of time, we arrive at a price target of $134, which is 22% higher than its current levels.

I would also like to point out that Crocs is trading at the lowest forward price-to-earnings multiple of 7x its FY25 earnings, compared to its peers, which include Skechers (NYSE:SKX) and Deckers Outdoor (DECK), which are trading at significantly higher multiples. While it is true that it is growing at a slower pace than its competitors, it, however, has the highest operating margin of them all, as can be seen below.

My final verdict and conclusions

Although revenue growth in the Crocs brand is expected to slow in FY25, along with margin contraction at a company level, I believe that it is already priced in. Unless we see a material deterioration in the overall demand landscape, I expect the company to meet or exceed its FY25 revenue guidance, which should position it for at least a 20% upside from its current levels.

As a result, I will reiterate my “buy” rating with a price target of $134.3.
